turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kennedy has our opinion in case 16-1371, Byrd v. United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Pennsylvania State Troopers pulled over a car driven by the petitioner. His name is Terrence Byrd. Byrd was the only person in the car. In the course of the traffic stop the troopers learned that the car was rented but that Byrd was not listed on the rental agreement as an authorized driver. For this reason, the troopers told Byrd that they did not need his consent to search the car, including the trunk where he had stored personal effects. A search of the trunk uncovered body armor and 49 bricks of heroin. After this evidence was turned over to federal authorities and Byrd was indicted on federal crimes, he moved to suppress the evidence as the fruit of an unlawful search. The United States District Court for the Middle District of Pennsylvania denied the motion and the Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit affirmed. The basic question confronting those courts and the question this Court now addresses is whether a person who is an unauthorized driver of a rental car can claim that his own fourth amendment rights were infringed by the search that he seeks to challenge. The Court now holds that, as a general rule, someone in otherwise lawful possession and control of a rental car has a reasonable expectation of privacy in it. This is true even if the rental agreement does not list him or her as an authorized driver. Although the Court has not set forth a single metric or exhaustive list of considerations to resolve the circumstances in which a person can be said to have a reasonable expectation of privacy, it has explained that expectations of privacy must have a source outside of the Fourth Amendment, this can be either by reference to concepts of real or personal property or to understandings that are recognized and permitted by society. In cases like this, when the two concepts are often linked. "One of the main rights attaching to property is the right to exclude others," in the main, "one who owns or lawfully possesses or controls property will have a legitimate expectation of privacy by virtue of the right to exclude." There is no reason why the expectation of privacy that comes from lawful possession and control and the attendant's right to exclude would differ depending on whether the car is rented or privately owned by someone other than the person in current lawful; possession of it. The key though is a requirement of lawful possession. No matter the degree of possession and control, a car thief would not have a reasonable expectation of privacy in a stolen car. On this point, the government asserts that on the facts here, Byrd had no greater expectation of privacy than a car thief. In brief, Byrd waited in the parking lot of the rental company while his friend went inside to rent the car. His friend did not list anyone else as a driver on the rental agreement if she immediately handed the keys to Byrd. From this, the government infers that Byrd intentionally used a third-party in a calculated plan to mislead the rental company from the very outset or to aid him in committing a crime. It may be that the law should not distinguish between one who obtains a vehicle through subterfuge of the type the government alleges occurred here and one steals the car outright. But the government did not raise this argument in the District Court of the Court of Appeals. It is unclear from the record whether the government's inferences paid an accurate picture of what occurred and the Court therefore declines to reach this argument. The proper course is to remand the case for this argument to be considered in the first instance, by the Court of Appeals or by the District Court. Those courts may also wish to consider whether probable cause justify the search in any event. The judgment of the Court of Appeals is reversed. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion in which Justice Gorsuch has joined. Justice Alito has filed a concurring opinion.
